articles.title: Open Thread
articles.content:
**沃洛赫阴谋**
多为法律学者 | 时有异见 | 常持自由意志主义 | 始终独立

帮助《理性》以更多扎实的新闻报道反击。您的一份支持意味着更多记者、更多调查、更多报道。今天就捐款！不了，谢谢

articles.title: Seattle's Sky-High Minimum Wage for Delivery Drivers Has Been a Disaster
articles.content:
不久之前，出现了一种新型工作：基于应用程序的零工经济工作。

这些工作包括Rover上的遛狗、Taskrabbit上的跑腿、DoorDash外卖配送、Uber和Lyft驾驶。

很多人喜欢零工工作。它很灵活。你想什么时候工作就什么时候工作。

但“工人权利”活动人士和执政的社会党人不喜欢这样。零工很少加入工会。他们没有最低工资保障。

“优步和Lyft剥削工人”是MS NOW的头条。“我们不能忽视这个问题。”

民主党社会主义者声称他们有解决方案。西雅图市议会实施了每小时26美元的配送司机最低工资。

能出什么岔子呢？

两年后，我们知道了答案：零工没有赚到更多钱，但价格却上涨了。

DoorDash和Uber Eats等应用向消费者收取5美元的费用，“以帮助抵消这些……法规的成本”。

现在西雅图居民抱怨价格问题。视频中的一位居民抱怨说：“我订了一份12美元的三明治……12美元涨到了32美元！”“我只是删掉了这个应用。”

一位应用司机抱怨说：“由于新法律，工作变得很慢。”DoorDash表示，2024年西雅图的订单减少了170万份。

这就是政客们规定工资的后果。

经济学教授朱德·格洛克表示：“显然，当企业成本增加时，客户的成本也会增加。这些市场复杂得令人难以想象，公司的主要工作是连接餐厅、配送工人和客户。然后，一个经济上不识字的市议会或市长认为，只要通过阅读新闻来审视一个行业，就可以恰当地规范具体的工资标准。”

前西雅图市议会主席萨拉·纳尔逊承认政客们犯了错误：“我们制造了一个问题，我们有责任解决它。”

他们废除了有害的法律吗？

没有。

纳尔逊说他们只需要调整数字：“如果我们把最低工资标准定对了，就不会看到收入下降。”

多么自负！政客阶层竟然认为自己知道每个工人应该拿多少工资。

价格管制从来不起作用。

灵活的定价才有效。

竞争迫使企业不断调整薪酬和价格，以吸引工人和客户。

格洛克说，当傲慢的政客们认为自己可以设定一个“正确的”价格时，“这简直是荒谬的。你不会为人们带来任何福祉的提升，也不会为那些工人增加工资。”

纽约市类似的最低工资上涨也失败了，此前政客们保证基于应用的司机每小时最低工资约20美元。

格洛克说：“小费和就业竞争的增加抵消了强制最低工资带来的所有收益。就是这样不断打地鼠的趋势。市场有反应，[所以政客们]通过新法规试图阻止这种反应。他们认为下一条法规会 somehow把贪婪从体系中剔除出去，但这是根本不可能做到的。”

竞争是决定人们薪酬的唯一好方法。

格洛克说：“很多政客相信有免费午餐，或者有一个固定的资金池可以分配给最需要帮助的人。实际效果不是改善工人的福祉，而是增加客户成本，破坏这座城市最成功的企业之一。”
articles.summary:
Not long ago, new kinds of jobs appeared: app-based gig work.

They include jobs like dog walking on Rover, Taskrabbit work, DoorDash food delivery, Uber and Lyft driving.

Lots of people like gig work. It's flexible. You work when you want to work.

But "workers' rights" activists and governing socialists don't like that. Gig workers rarely join unions. They don't get a minimum wage.

"Uber and Lyft exploit their workers" is a headline at MS NOW. "We can't ignore it."

The Democratic Socialists said they had a solution. Seattle's city council imposed a $26 delivery driver minimum wage.

What could go wrong?

Two years later, we know the answer: Gig workers make no more money, but prices went up.

Apps like DoorDash and Uber Eats added a $5 fee for consumers "to help cover the costs of these…regulations."

Now Seattle residents complain about prices. "I ordered a $12 sandwich…$12 grew to $32!" complains one in my new video. "I just deleted the app."

"[Work] has become slow because of the new law," an app driver complains. DoorDash says it got 1.7 million fewer Seattle orders in 2024.

This is what happens when politicians dictate wages.

"Obviously, when you're increasing cost to businesses, you're going to increase costs to customers," says economics professor Judge Glock. "These are unimaginably complicated markets where the company's main job is interfacing between restaurants and delivery workers and customers. Then you have an economically illiterate city council or mayor who thinks, basically by looking at an industry through reading the news, they can appropriately regulate the exact wage."

Former Seattle City Council President Sara Nelson admits that the politicians made a mistake: "We created a problem and it's our responsibility to fix it."

They repealed the harmful law?

No.

Nelson said they just needed to adjust their numbers: "If we had gotten the minimum pay standard right, we would not see the decline in the revenue."

Such conceit! Somehow, the political class knows exactly what every worker should be paid.

Price controls never work.

Flexible pricing does.

Competition forces businesses to constantly adjust pay and prices to attract workers and customers.

When smug politicians think they can set a price that's "right," "it's just patently absurd," says Glock. "You're not going to have any improved well-being for people, and you're not going to have increased wages for those workers."

A similar minimum wage increase failed in New York City, after politicians guaranteed app-based drivers an hourly minimum of about $20.

"The decrease in tips and increased competition for jobs offset all of the gains from that imposed minimum wage," says Glock. "It's this continual whack-a-mole tendency. The market responds, [so politicians] pass a new regulation to try to prevent that response. They think the next regulation will somehow squelch the greed out of the system, but there's simply no way to do that."

Competition is the only good way to decide what people get paid.

"A lot of politicians believe there's a free lunch or a fixed pot of money that they can give out to the neediest people." says Glock. "The actual effect was not to improve the well-being of workers, but to increase costs for customers and sabotage one of the most successful businesses in the city."

COPYRIGHT 2026 BY JFS PRODUCTIONS INC.

The post Seattle's Sky-High Minimum Wage for Delivery Drivers Has Been a Disaster appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: SCOTUS Unanimously Ruled That the Second Amendment Trumps Anti-Drug Sentiment
articles.content:
最高法院历来通过削弱公民自由来推动禁毒战争，批评者长期以来一直认为《权利法案》存在一个“毒品例外”。但上周，当大法官们一致维护大麻消费者持有枪支的权利时，他们明确表示，《第二修正案》不存在毒品例外。

最高法院的一致判决反映出联邦法律的明显荒谬——该法律将“任何受管制物质”的“非法使用者”持有枪支定为重罪，可判处最高15年监禁。这一政策与最高法院在《第二修正案》判决中的指导原则——“这个国家枪支管制的历史传统”——明显不一致，以至于各政治派别的法官和组织联合起来谴责这一政策。

该案涉及德克萨斯州男子阿里·赫马尼，他承认自己拥有一把手枪并每周使用几次大麻，这足以让他被判非法持有枪支罪。但一名联邦法官以《第二修正案》为由驳回了指控，美国第五巡回上诉法院维持了这一裁决。

第五巡回法院通常被认为是美国最保守的联邦上诉法院，该法院表示，这一结果与其先前的结论一致——《第二修正案》禁止仅基于法定要素的此类起诉。特朗普政府尽管宣称致力于“保护《第二修正案》权利”，却要求最高法院驳回第五巡回法院的推理，恢复对赫马尼的指控。

此案双方都出现了奇特的组合。出乎意料的是，已将娱乐性大麻合法化的18个蓝色州的检察长加入了特朗普政府的阵营，敦促最高法院允许对赫马尼的起诉，显然是因为他们认为保护枪支管制比捍卫大麻应像酒精一样被对待的原则更重要。

特朗普政府的立场引发了全国步枪协会、其他主要《第二修正案》团体以及几个自由意志主义组织（包括理性基金会）的强烈反对。他们与禁毒联盟、全国大麻法律改革组织和全国刑事辩护律师协会结盟。

更引人注目的是：美国公民自由联盟此前一直主张《第二修正案》不保障个人持有武器的权利，此次却加入了赫马尼提交给最高法院的简报，明确捍卫这一权利。反对起诉赫马尼的这个意识形态多元联盟生动地说明了在枪支管制和禁毒战争交叉点上跨党派联盟的潜力。

特朗普政府的论点关键依赖于一个显然不恰当的类比——将大麻消费者与“习惯性酒鬼”进行对比，后者根据流浪法和民事拘禁令，可以被关进监狱、劳改所或精神病院。大法官们毫不费力地认识到这一类比的谬误。

“政府在此依赖的习惯性酒鬼法律在政府邀请我们考虑的每一个标准上都与指控赫马尼的法律截然不同，”尼尔·戈萨奇大法官在多数意见书中指出。“它们针对不同类型的人，出于不同的目的，以不同的方式运作。”

最高法院保留了这样一种可能性：当有额外证据表明持有枪支的吸毒者对公共安全构成威胁时，可以起诉他们。但法院得出结论，政府不能仅仅因为人们是大麻使用者就剥夺他们的《第二修正案》权利或起诉他们非法持有枪支。

这一影响范围广泛，因为调查数据显示，约有2000万美国大麻消费者拥有枪支。尽管每年只有极小比例的潜在被告被起诉，但这意味着运气不好的人可能因违反一项任意、被广泛违反且执行随意的法律而锒铛入狱。

该法律将不侵犯任何人权利的行为定为犯罪，这是枪支法和毒品法一贯的做法。最高法院的这一判决是纠正这一不公正的微小但值得欢迎的一步。

© 2026年创作者辛迪加公司版权所有
本文首次发表于Reason.com，标题为《最高法院一致裁定第二修正案胜过反毒情绪》
articles.summary:
The Supreme Court has a history of facilitating the war on drugs by whittling away at civil liberties, to the point that critics have long perceived a "drug exception" to the Bill of Rights. But last week, when the justices unanimously upheld the gun rights of cannabis consumers, they made it clear that there is no drug exception to the Second Amendment.

The Court's consensus reflects the blatant illogic of a federal law that makes it a felony, punishable by up to 15 years in prison, for an "unlawful user" of "any controlled substance" to possess a firearm. That policy is so clearly inconsistent with "this Nation's historical tradition of firearm regulation," the lodestar of the Court's Second Amendment decisions, that jurists and organizations across the political spectrum united in condemning it.

The case involved Ali Hemani, a Texas man who admitted he owned a pistol and used marijuana a few times a week, which would have been enough to convict him of illegal gun possession. But a federal judge dismissed the charge on Second Amendment grounds, and the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit upheld that decision.

The 5th Circuit, which is often described as the country's most conservative federal appeals court, said that result was consistent with its prior conclusion that the Second Amendment bars such prosecutions when they are based on nothing more than the statutory elements. The Trump administration, despite its avowed commitment to "protecting Second Amendment rights," asked the Supreme Court to reject the 5th Circuit's reasoning and reinstate the charge against Hemani.

The case featured strange bedfellows on both sides. Counterintuitively, the attorneys general of 18 blue states that have legalized recreational marijuana joined the Trump administration in urging the Supreme Court to allow Hemani's prosecution, apparently because they thought protecting gun control was more important than defending the principle that cannabis should be treated like alcohol.

The Trump administration's position provoked vigorous objections from the National Rifle Association, other leading Second Amendment groups, and several libertarian organizations (including Reason Foundation). They were allied with the Drug Policy Alliance, the National Organization for the Reform of Marijuana Laws, and the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers.

Even more striking: The American Civil Liberties Union, which had long maintained that the Second Amendment does not guarantee an individual right to arms, joined Hemani's Supreme Court brief, which explicitly defended that right. The ideologically diverse coalition opposing Hemani's prosecution vividly illustrated the potential for transpartisan alliances at the intersection of gun control and the war on drugs.

The Trump administration's case hinged on a plainly inapt comparison between cannabis consumers and "habitual drunkards," who historically could be confined to jails, workhouses, or asylums under vagrancy and civil commitment laws. The justices had no trouble recognizing the fallaciousness of that analogy.

"The habitual drunkard laws on which the government relies here differ dramatically" from the law under which Hemani was charged "on every single metric the government invites us to consider," Justice Neil Gorsuch noted in the majority opinion. "They targeted different kinds of people, did so for different purposes, and operated in different ways."

The Court left open the possibility that gun-owning drug users could be prosecuted when there is additional evidence that they pose a threat to public safety. But it concluded that the government may not strip people of their Second Amendment rights or prosecute them for illegal gun possession simply because they are marijuana users.

The implications are broad, since survey data suggest that something like 20 million American cannabis consumers own guns. Although only a tiny percentage of potential defendants are prosecuted each year, that means bad luck can send people to prison for violating an arbitrary, widely flouted, and haphazardly enforced law.

That law criminalizes conduct that violates no one's rights, as gun and drug laws routinely do. The Supreme Court's decision is a modest but welcome step toward rectifying that injustice.

© Copyright 2026 by Creators Syndicate Inc.

The post SCOTUS Unanimously Ruled That the Second Amendment Trumps Anti-Drug Sentiment appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Authorship Predictions For The Remaining Cases
articles.content:
今天最高法院很忙。大法官们发布了五份判决意见书，虽然都不是重大案件。我已经读完了Cisco、Exxon和Pung的判决。其他两份稍后再读。在此，我想对未决案件的撰写人做一个预测。这些猜测值你付的价钱。（而且与越来越多的终身制法学教授不同，我的博文不收取订阅费。）

让我们按庭期逐一分析。

十月庭期共辩论了十个案件，全部已判决。杰克逊大法官有两份意见书，其他大法官各一份。

十一月庭期辩论了九个案件，全部已判决。卡瓦诺和杰克逊大法官没有撰写意见书。戈萨奇和巴雷特大法官各有两份。

十二月庭期辩论了八个案件。其中五个已判决，Hamm v. Smith案被撤销。还有两个案件未决：Trump v. Slaughter案和NRSC v. FEC案。目前为止，托马斯、卡根、戈萨奇、巴雷特和杰克逊大法官都撰写了十二月的意见书。首席大法官罗伯茨以及阿里托、索托马约尔和卡瓦诺大法官尚未撰写。五月份时，我推测阿里托大法官在Hamm案中失去了多数意见书的撰写权。我预测首席大法官罗伯茨将撰写Slaughter案，卡瓦诺大法官将撰写NRSC案。我认为索托马约尔大法官没有该庭期的意见书。

一月庭期辩论了七个案件。其中三个已由托马斯、索托马约尔和杰克逊大法官撰写。还有四个案件未决，而且都是大案。首先，我有相当把握首席大法官罗伯茨将撰写Cook案。这是他肯定会亲自把控的案件类型。但既然杰罗姆·鲍威尔不再担任美联储主席，Cook案似乎不再那么热门。我猜测某位大法官将同时撰写Little v. Hecox案和W.V. v. B.P.J.案的单一意见书。我最看好的是卡瓦诺大法官，他对女性体育运动有特别关注；或者是巴雷特大法官，她是《教育法修正案第九条》的直接受益者。唯一的变数是卡根大法官，她对《第九条》的传统观点持同情态度，可能会找到一种方式撰写一份说服首席大法官的狭窄多数意见书。卡根本任期只有五份多数意见书，而索托马约尔和杰克逊都有六份。卡根该写一份重要意见书了。戈萨奇大法官撰写了Hemani案，但我认为他不会同时撰写Wolford案。戈萨奇已经有七份了。我的猜测是，不撰写这两起跨性别体育案件的大法官将撰写Wolford案。

二月庭期辩论了七个案件，全部已判决。首席大法官罗伯茨和杰克逊大法官没有撰写该庭期的意见书。

三月庭期辩论了八个案件，其中五个已判决。目前为止，首席大法官罗伯茨以及托马斯、阿里托和巴雷特大法官尚未撰写三月的意见书。我再次认为首席大法官罗伯茨将亲自撰写Trump v. Barbara案。这个案件太重要了，不会放手。Watson v. RNC案涉及迟到选票能否被计入。如果最高法院维持第五巡回法院的判决，我认为阿里托大法官将撰写意见书；如果最高法院推翻第五巡回法院的判决，巴雷特大法官将撰写意见书。Mullin v. Al Otro Lado案涉及在墨西哥一侧被拘留并寻求庇护的外籍人士。我怀疑巴雷特或阿里托——也就是不撰写Watson案的那位——将撰写Mullin案。

四月庭期辩论了九个案件，其中六个已公布。阿里托、卡根和卡瓦诺大法官尚未撰写四月的意见书。三个未决案件是：Monsanto案、Chatrie案和Mullin v. Doe案。这些案件的分配有点难，因为我不确定这些案件的结果。Monsanto案辩论时，我认为化工公司很难凑够五票。阿里托和卡瓦诺是支持优先权的坚定票。如果他们有多数票，那么罗伯茨和/或巴雷特也投票支持了优先权。否则，就是卡根撰写了多数意见书。Chatrie案是关于地理围栏的第四修正案案件。我能想象卡根或卡瓦诺获得这个案件。Doe案涉及海地人、叙利亚人和其他群体的临时保护身份被中止。我能想象卡根或卡瓦诺获得这个案件。不过，海地原告已请求最高法院撤销该案，所以可能有人失去了多数意见书的撰写权。我认为撤销的可能性不大。副总检察长的回复是，新发现的文件与本案所涉问题无关。此外，这些文件不像2020年人口普查案中的提交材料那样具有爆炸性。这些记录显示（令人震惊地！），政治任命官员制定政策。这一基本观点可能让深层国家的人不安，但这反映了治理的现实。参见Slaughter案。

看看周四会带来什么。
articles.summary:
Today was a busy day at the Court. The Justices handed down five opinions, though none were major. I've read Cisco, Exxon, and Pung. I'll finish the other two later. Here, I'd like to venture authorship predictions for the outstanding cases. These guesses are worth what you paid for them. (And unlike an increasing number of tenured law professors, I do not charge a subscription fee for my blog posts.)

Let's go sitting-by-sitting.

Ten cases were argued in the October sitting and all ten were decided. Justice Jackson had two opinions, and the other Justices each had one.

Nine cases were argued in the November sitting and all nine were decided. Justices Kavanaugh and Jackson had none. Justices Gorsuch and Barrett each had two.

Eight cases were argued in the December sitting. Five of those cases were already decided, and Hamm v. Smith was DIG'd. Two cases remain outstanding: Trump v. Slaughter and NRSC v. FEC. So far, Justices Thomas, Kagan, Gorsuch, Barrett, and Jackson have written for December. The Chief Justice and Justices Alito, Sotomayor, and Kavanaugh have not yet written.  In May, I speculated that Justice Alito lost the majority opinion in Hamm. I predict that Chief Justice Roberts has Slaughter and Justice Kavanaugh has NRSC. I do not think Justice Sotomayor has an opinion from the sitting.

Seven cases were argued in the January sitting. Three of those cases were authored by Justices Thomas, Sotomayor, and Jackson. Four cases remain outstanding, and they are all huge. First, I am reasonably confident Chief Justice Roberts will write Cook. This is the sort of case he will certainly keep for himself. But now that Jerome Powell is no longer Chairman of the Fed, Cook seems must less hot. I suspect one Justice will write a single opinion for both Little v. Hecox and W.V. v. B.P.J. My best guess is Coach Kavanaugh, who has a special interest in female athletics, or Justice Barrett, who was a direct beneficiary of Title IX. The one wildcard will be if Justice Kagan, who was sympathetic to traditional view of Title IX, finds a way to write some narrow majority opinion that convinced the Chief. Kagan only has five majority opinions for the term, while Sotomayor and Jackson both have six. Kagan is due for a big one. Justice Gorusch wrote Hemani, but I don't think he also writes Wolford. Gorsuch is already at seven for the term. My guess is that whoever doesn't write the transgender sports cases will write Wolford.

Seven cases were argued in February. All seven cases were already decided. Chief Justice Roberts and Justice Jackson did not write from that sitting.

Eight cases were argued in March. Five were already decided. So far, Chief Justice Roberts, and Justices Thomas, Alito, and Barrett have not written for March. Once again, I think Chief Justice Roberts will keep Trump v. Barbara for himself. This case is too important to give up. Watson v. RNC considers whether late-arriving ballots can be counted. If the Court affirms the Fifth Circuit, I think Justice Alito has the opinion; if the Court reverses the Fifth Circuit, Justice Barrett will have the opinion. Mullin v. Al Otro Lado concerns aliens who are detained on the Mexico-side of the border and seek asylum. I suspect Barrett or Alito--whoever does not write Watson--will have Watson.

Nine cases were argued in April, and six were already released. Justice Alito, Kagan, and Kavanaugh have not yet written for April. The three outstanding cases are: Monsanto, Chatrie, and Mullin v. Doe. These assignments are a bit tricky to make since I am not sure how these cases will turn out. When Monsanto was argued, I thought it would be tough for the chemical company to count to five. Alito and Kavanaugh are solid votes for preemption. If they have the majority, then Roberts and/or Barrett voted for preemption. But if not, Kagan has the majority opinion. Chartrie is a Fourth Amendment case about geofences. I could see Kagan or Kavanaugh getting this case as well. And Doe concerns suspension of Temporary Protected Status for Haitians, Syrians, and other groups. I could see Kagan or Kavanaugh getting this one. Then again, the Haitian plaintiffs have asked the Court to DIG this case, so someone may have lost a majority opinion. I think a DIG is unlikely. The SG replied that the newfound documents do not concern the issue at play in this case. Moreover, these documents are not blockbusters like the submissions in the 2020 census case. The records seem to show (shockingly!) that political appointees make policy decisions. This basic point may upset those in the Deep State, but reflects a reality of governance. See Slaughter.

Let's see what Thursday brings.

The post Authorship Predictions For The Remaining Cases appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Joy Reid: "Nobody Black I Know Is Really Excited About the Fourth of July"

articles.summary:
On this segment of Free Media, Senior Editor Robby Soave and Daily Caller Editor in Chief Amber Duke discuss why former MSNBC host Joy Reid may think that the American Revolution is not worth celebrating.

The post Joy Reid: "Nobody Black I Know Is Really Excited About the Fourth of July" appeared first on Reason.com.
